exercises.prompt: Implement add(a, b) so it returns the sum of the two numbers a and b.
exercises.solution:
def add(a, b):
    return a + b
hints: A Python function hands a value back only with the return keyword — an untouched stub runs pass and returns None, so every test fails.
The + operator adds two numbers: a + b.
The whole body is one line: return a + b (delete the pass).

exercises.prompt: Implement biggest(a, b) so it returns the larger of the two numbers. If they are equal, return that shared value.
exercises.solution:
def biggest(a, b):
    return a if a > b else b
hints: Compare the two values with the greater-than operator: a > b.
Python's conditional expression a if a > b else b evaluates to a when a is bigger and b otherwise — a one-line if/else.
When a and b are equal, a > b is False, so the expression returns b (which equals a) — the right shared value.

exercises.prompt: Implement fizzbuzz(n). Return 'Fizz' if n is divisible by 3, 'Buzz' if divisible by 5, 'FizzBuzz' if divisible by both, and otherwise the number itself as a string — fizzbuzz(7) returns '7'.
hints: The remainder operator % tells you divisibility: n % 3 == 0 is True exactly when n is a multiple of 3.
Check the both-divisible case first — n % 15 == 0 — otherwise 15 matches 'Fizz' and returns before reaching 'FizzBuzz'.
For the fall-through case, convert the number to text with str(n) so you return '7', not the integer 7.
